Renormalization Group Flow as Optimal Transport

Reference

Renormalization Group Flow as Optimal Transport

Introduction

I have very little knowledge in physics, so there might be many errors in this post. If you see any errors, please point it out in the comment!

Renormalization theory, to put it simply, is a theory describing that every theory is the optimal theory under our capability of understanding, thus might be only effective to some extent.

Ising Model

The Lattice

A -dimensional lattice is the set of all integer points in -dimensional Euclidean space . To make it easier to deal with, we usually take a subset and put periodic boundary conditions on (like the Snake game).

Every point in the lattice is called a site.

For every two neighbors , if , we call them nearest neighbors, denoted as .

Configuration Space

For every site , we have a state , where is the set of all possible states of the system.

A configuration is a function , which means every site has a state .

The configuration space is the set of all possible configurations, i.e., , where is the number of sites in .

Operators

An operator is a functional , which means it takes a configuration and returns a real number.

The most important operator is the Hamiltonian , which describes the energy of a configuration .

Symmetry

We may constrain the possible configurations by some symmetry.

We say a physical system with state space , law of dynamics and observables has a symmetry if for any observable , we have for all .

So we can use the symmetry to reduce the number of operators we need to consider.

For the Ising model, we mainly focus on two operators:

Magnetization Operator

Nearest-Neighbor Interaction Operator

The Hamiltonian is defined as a linear combination over a basis of the linear vector space .

For the Ising model,

where are coupling constants describing the strength of the interaction and the external field.

When the external field is zero everywhere, , the Ising model is symmetric under switching the value of the spin in all the lattice sites; a nonzero field breaks this symmetry.

The Partition Function

The partition function is a function of the Hamiltonian and the temperature , defined as:

The configuration probability is given by the Boltzmann distribution where , and is the Boltzmann constant.

Continuous Generalization

From Lattice to Continuum

Let the distance between two points approach , then becomes a real scalar field on .

The Hamiltonian becomes the action functional as the integral of the Lagrangian density

The partition function becomes the functional integral:

Propagator

Definition

After defining the behavior of the field and its statistical distribution , we shall ask the fundamental question—What is the relation between the different point in a field.

In other words, if I know the value at point , what can I say about the value at another point ?

We introduce the propagator (also Green’s function, two-point correlation function)

as the expectation value of the product of the fields at two different points.

In the momentum space, the propagator is defined as:

The relation is given by the Fourier transform:

Let ：

The Source Field Method (as Partitional Generating Function)

Calculate the propagator directly from the definition is very complicated due to the presence of interactions.

For the free field (no interactions, ), the action is quadratic:

If we calculate the propagator directly, we get:

To calculate this propagator, we need to evaluate the Gaussian integrals involved.

But actually, we can use the technique of generating function.

Recall that, for a random variable , we can define its generating function as:

And its moment can be obtained by differentiating times and evaluating at :

Now we are dealing with a field and functional partition function .

Similarly, we introduce a source field and define the partition function with source as: or, in momentum space Take the functional derivative of , we obtain: And evaluate it at , this is exactly the definition of propagator: where .

For the free field, where .

This substitution is called the completing the square technique.

The propagator is:

This gives the familiar result that in position space, the two-point correlation function is:

Connection to Exact RG

This momentum space formulation naturally leads to the exact renormalization group approach, where we systematically integrate out high-momentum modes while keeping track of how the effective action changes. The cutoff function mentioned in the paper provides a smooth way to suppress high-momentum modes above the scale .
